_work.title: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The visual tradition of Protestant mystical theosophy descending from Jacob Böhme (1575–1624), the 'Teutonic philosopher' of Görlitz: his portraits and the symbolic plates that Dionysius Andreas Freher engraved for William Law's English edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen*; Johann Georg Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica* with its figure of regenerate and natural man and his planetary soul-centres; and the wider Behmenist current — John Pordage and the Philadelphian Society, Antoinette Bourignon, Quirinus Kuhlmann, and Emanuel Swedenborg's New Church of heaven, hell, and correspondences.

entries.title: Portrait of Jakob Böhme
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous (after a 17th-century likeness); Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraved memorial portrait of Jakob Böhme (1575–1624), the Lutheran shoemaker of Görlitz whose visionary writings founded the tradition of Protestant theosophy.

## Iconography
A bust-length likeness of a lean, bearded man in a plain coat and broad ruffed collar, set within an oval or rectangular frame. The sober dress and direct gaze present him as a humble artisan rather than a learned doctor, the convention by which later editions canonised the 'Teutonic philosopher.'

## Significance
Böhme's vision of God's self-revelation out of the [[motif:the unground (ungrund)|Ungrund]] through opposing principles of wrath and love made him the fountainhead of Behmenism. Suppressed in his lifetime, his works were gathered in the great Amsterdam editions and carried across Europe by Gichtel, Pordage, and later William Law, shaping Pietism, Romanticism, and modern esotericism. Portraits like this prefaced those collected works as a frontispiece-icon of the inspired layman.

## For artists & game designers
The template for the 'humble visionary' archetype — a tradesman whose plain dress signals that wisdom came by grace, not schooling.

entries.title: Jakob Böhme Writing One of His Books
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraving showing Jakob Böhme seated at his desk in the act of writing, the visual claim that his books were dictated by inward illumination.

## Iconography
The theosopher sits before an open book with pen in hand, often with a beam or radiance of divine light falling upon him from above. The interior of the study and the gesture of writing mark him as a scribe of revelation rather than an inventor of doctrine.

## Significance
Böhme described his first illumination of 1600 — the famous reflection of sunlight in a pewter dish that opened the 'centre of nature' to him — as the source of *Aurora* and all that followed. Images of him writing under heavenly light dramatise the Behmenist conviction that true theosophy is *Erkenntnis*, a knowing given from within rather than acquired by study. The motif aligns him with the inspired evangelists and prophets of older Christian iconography.

## For artists & game designers
A ready emblem of channelled writing: the scribe whose words arrive as light from outside the frame.

entries.title: Title Engraving for *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (Freher)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Dionysius Andreas Freher
entries.summary:
A symbolic plate by Dionysius Andreas Freher (1649–1728) prepared for the English edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen, the Teutonic Theosopher*.

## Iconography
The sheet sets out Böhme's cosmology in diagrammatic form — concentric or paired figures distinguishing the dark fire-world from the light love-world, with the principles of eternal nature labelled and explained. Freher's plates are designed to be 'read,' each part keyed to a passage of doctrine.

## Significance
Freher, a German émigré in London, devoted decades to clarifying Böhme for English readers; his manuscript treatises and engraved figures were taken up by William Law for the four-volume edition of 1764–1781. These plates are the most ambitious attempt to render the [[motif:three principles of divine being|three principles]] visible, turning difficult theosophy into a sequence of contemplative emblems. They became the standard visual vocabulary of English Behmenism.

## For artists & game designers
A model for the 'annotated cosmos' interface — a diagram whose every zone carries a legend explaining its metaphysical function.

citations.text: Désirée Hirst, *Hidden Riches: Traditional Symbolism from the Renaissance to Blake* (1964).

entries.title: Symbolic Plate from *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (Fleuron T125516-46)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Dionysius Andreas Freher (designer); after Jakob Böhme
entries.summary:
One of the engraved symbolic figures from William Law's English edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen, the Teutonic Theosopher* (1764–1781), designed after Freher.

## Iconography
A self-contained emblematic plate mapping a stage of Böhme's eternal nature — typically paired or concentric forms dividing wrath from love, fire from light, with the soul's place marked at the meeting of the principles. Lettered keys tie each element to the accompanying text.

## Significance
The Law edition reproduced a numbered series of these plates so the reader could hold the whole architecture of [[motif:fall and new birth|fall and regeneration]] before the eye. They distil decades of Freher's labour to make Böhme intelligible, and through this edition they reached William Blake and the Romantic visionaries.

## For artists & game designers
A tile in a larger diagrammatic deck: one self-contained cosmological state meant to be compared against its neighbours.

entries.title: Symbolic Plate from *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (Fleuron T125516-47)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Dionysius Andreas Freher (designer); after Jakob Böhme
entries.summary:
Another engraved figure from the William Law edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (1764–1781), part of Freher's explanatory series.

## Iconography
A circular or wheel-like diagram of the qualities of eternal nature, the seven source-spirits (*Quellgeister*) arranged so that their turning generates the tension of fire and light. Lettered captions identify the properties and the points of the soul's fall and rebirth.

## Significance
Böhme conceived nature as a [[motif:three principles of divine being|dynamic of properties]] perpetually birthing themselves from the Ungrund; Freher's wheel makes that motion legible. The plate belongs to the apparatus that allowed English readers to follow Böhme's notoriously dense prose, and it transmitted his imagery of the rotating qualities to later occult cosmology.

## For artists & game designers
A rotating 'qualities wheel' — a dial of opposed forces whose turning drives the state of the whole system.

entries.title: Symbolic Plate from *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (Fleuron T125516-49)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Dionysius Andreas Freher (designer); after Jakob Böhme
entries.summary:
A further symbolic plate from the William Law edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (1764–1781), continuing Freher's diagrammatic exposition.

## Iconography
The figure pictures the regeneration of the soul — the [[motif:fall and new birth|new birth]] in which the dark fire is overcome by the in-breaking light of the heart of God. Lettered keys mark the stations of the descent into wrath and the ascent into grace.

## Significance
The doctrine of the new birth (*Wiedergeburt*) is the practical heart of Böhme's theosophy: salvation as an inward alchemical transmutation of the will. Freher's plate gives this process a map, one of the devotional images that made Behmenism a path of contemplation as well as a body of speculation. Through Law's edition it reached Pietist and Romantic readers alike.

## For artists & game designers
A visual 'redemption arc' diagram — a single figure tracking a soul's fall into shadow and return to light.

entries.title: Symbolic Plate from *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (Fleuron T125516-62)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Dionysius Andreas Freher (designer); after Jakob Böhme
entries.summary:
A late plate in the symbolic series of the William Law edition of *The Works of Jacob Behmen* (1764–1781), one of Freher's most elaborate figures.

## Iconography
The sheet builds a layered globe or sphere of eternal nature, the dark and light worlds nested with the abyss below and the crown of glory above. Densely lettered, it gathers many of the doctrine's elements into a single comprehensive emblem.

## Significance
Where the held *Philosophische Kugel* condenses Böhme's vision into one eye-shaped globe, the Law-edition series unfolds it across many plates; this figure is among the summary diagrams that draw the strands together. It exemplifies Freher's conviction that the whole of [[motif:three principles of divine being|theosophy]] could in principle be displayed to the eye, a faith in diagrammatic revelation that runs through the Behmenist tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A 'master map' end-screen: the layered world-sphere that gathers every previously seen fragment into one comprehensible whole.

entries.title: The Regenerate Man (*Wiedergeborener Mensch*), Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica*
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Johann Georg Gichtel
entries.summary:
The famous figure of the regenerate man from Johann Georg Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica*, charting the planetary centres of the human soul.

## Iconography
A standing human body is overlaid with the signs of the seven planets placed at successive centres — Saturn at the feet or root, the Sun or the heart of Christ at the breast, the others ranged along the trunk. In the regenerate state a spiralling serpent of the dark world is bound and the centres are reordered around the indwelling Christ.

## Significance
Gichtel (1638–1710), Böhme's most devoted Amsterdam disciple and editor of the collected *Werke*, drew this diagram to picture an inward astrology: the planets are not stars but [[motif:planetary soul-centres|forces in the soul]] that the natural man suffers and the reborn man masters. The figure is one of the earliest European subtle-body maps and is often compared with later chakra systems, though Gichtel's frame is strictly Behmenist and Christian.

## For artists & game designers
A seven-node character sheet: planetary 'organs' that can be either corrupting drives or mastered powers depending on the soul's state.

citations.text: Arthur Versluis, *Wisdom's Children: A Christian Esoteric Tradition* (1999).

entries.title: Soul Diagram from Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica*
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Johann Georg Gichtel
entries.summary:
A diagram of the human soul-body from Johann Georg Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica*, the companion to his figure of regenerate and natural man.

## Iconography
The outline of a human figure carries the planetary signs disposed along the centreline of the body, with the serpent of the dark world coiled through the unregenerate state. The arrangement contrasts the natural man, ruled by the wandering planets, with the reborn man whose centres are gathered around the heart.

## Significance
Gichtel's *Theosophia Practica* circulated as the practical handbook of Behmenist devotion. Its diagrams transpose Böhme's abstract [[motif:three principles of divine being|principles]] onto the body, so that spiritual rebirth becomes a re-ordering of inner planetary forces. Reproduced across the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, these images became a touchstone for esoteric anatomy and were later read — anachronistically — through the lens of yogic chakra theory.

## For artists & game designers
A before/after body diagram: the same avatar shown corrupted and redeemed, its inner 'stat nodes' rearranged.

entries.title: Portrait of John Pordage
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraved portrait of John Pordage (1607–1681), the English physician and theosopher who led the early Behmenist circle that became the Philadelphian Society.

## Iconography
A bust-length portrait within an oval, the sitter shown as a grave divine in clerical or scholarly dress. The framing and inscription present him as an authority of the English theosophic current.

## Significance
Pordage, rector of Bradfield and a student of Böhme, gathered around him the visionary group later organised by Jane Lead as the [[motif:philadelphian society|Philadelphian Society]]. His treatises — *Theologia Mystica*, *Sophia* — translated Behmenist theosophy into English and influenced Gichtel, who corresponded with the circle. This portrait belongs to the iconography by which the movement remembered its founders.

## For artists & game designers
The 'mystic physician' archetype: a respectable doctor-cleric who is secretly the head of a visionary brotherhood.

entries.title: Portrait of Antoinette Bourignon
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraved portrait of the Flemish mystic and religious reformer Antoinette Bourignon (1616–1680).

## Iconography
A bust-length likeness within an oval frame, the sitter in plain devotional dress, presented as a sober spiritual authority. The image circulated with her voluminous writings.

## Significance
Bourignon was a fierce prophetess of inward, undenominational Christianity who gathered followers across the Low Countries and, controversially, in Scotland, where 'Bourignonism' was condemned by the Kirk into the eighteenth century. Though independent of Böhme, she belongs to the same early-modern current of [[motif:correspondences of heaven and hell|interior, visionary religion]] that overlaps the Behmenist and Pietist world, and Gichtel knew of her circle. Her portrait is part of the broad theosophic iconography of inspired lay teachers.

## For artists & game designers
A fierce visionary matriarch — the prophet whose authority rests entirely on inner experience and who answers to no church.

entries.title: Portrait of Antoinette de Bourignon (RP-P-OB-51.082)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
A second engraved portrait of Antoinette Bourignon (1616–1680), a larger and differently framed impression held at the Rijksmuseum.

## Iconography
The prophetess is shown bust-length in plain dress, here within a more elaborately ornamented surround that may carry an inscription naming her and her cause. The high-resolution plate preserves the fine stipple and line work of the engraving.

## Significance
Multiple portrait states attest to Bourignon's celebrity as a religious figurehead whose printed works and image spread her message of a purely inward Christianity. As with the companion impression, the image situates her within the wider early-modern world of [[motif:correspondences of heaven and hell|visionary lay religion]] adjacent to Böhme's theosophy and Gichtel's Amsterdam network.

## For artists & game designers
Useful as an alternate portrait state — the same character rendered in a grander frame for a 'venerated founder' presentation.

entries.title: Portrait of Quirinus Kuhlmann
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraved portrait of the ecstatic poet and Behmenist prophet Quirinus Kuhlmann (1651–1689).

## Iconography
A bust-length likeness, the sitter presented as a youthful man of letters. The plate accompanied or commemorated his visionary poetry.

## Significance
Kuhlmann read Böhme as the herald of a coming 'Kühlmonarchy,' a millenarian kingdom of the Spirit, and poured the vision into his ecstatic *Kühlpsalter*. His prophetic travels carried him from London and Amsterdam to Constantinople and finally Moscow, where he was burned as a heretic in 1689. His portrait is part of the [[motif:fall and new birth|prophetic]] wing of the theosophic tradition, where Behmenist ideas turned into apocalyptic activism.

## For artists & game designers
The doomed visionary poet — a charismatic prophet whose certainty of a coming kingdom leads him to a martyr's fire.

citations.text: Walter Dietze, *Quirinus Kuhlmann: Ketzer und Poet* (1963).

entries.title: Portrait of Emanuel Swedenborg (Rijksmuseum)
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Anonymous; Rijksmuseum impression
entries.summary:
An engraved portrait of Emanuel Swedenborg (1688–1772), the Swedish scientist who became seer of heaven and hell and founder of the New Church.

## Iconography
A bust-length likeness in a wig and formal coat, the sitter presented as a dignified man of the Enlightenment — the visual register of a respected scientist rather than a wild prophet.

## Significance
After a distinguished career as an assessor of mines and natural philosopher, Swedenborg underwent a spiritual crisis in the 1740s and reported daily converse with angels and spirits. His doctrine of [[motif:correspondences of heaven and hell|correspondences]] — that every natural thing answers to a spiritual reality — and his account of the *Maximus Homo* or Grand Man, the heavens in human form, made him a central figure of modern esotericism, read closely by Blake and the Romantics. His sober portraits underline that this visionary was a credentialed scientist.

## For artists & game designers
The rational scientist who becomes a literal traveller of the afterlife — credibility and the uncanny in one figure.

entries.title: Emanuel Swedenborg, Stipple Engraving by C. & A. Paos
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: C. & A. Paos
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0, Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A stipple engraving of Emanuel Swedenborg (1688–1772) by C. & A. Paos, held in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
A bust-length stippled portrait, the fine dotted technique giving a soft tonal likeness of the seer in formal dress. The Wellcome impression preserves the plate at high resolution.

## Significance
The persistence of Swedenborg portraits into the nineteenth century reflects the spread of the New Church and of Swedenborgian ideas through Romanticism, Spiritualism, and the wider occult revival. As a complement to the eighteenth-century engravings, this later print shows how the image of the [[motif:correspondences of heaven and hell|Swedish seer]] was continually re-issued for new generations of readers.

## For artists & game designers
A softer, more reverent portrait state — the founder remembered and idealised a century after his death.

entries.title: Title Page of Swedenborg's *Arcana Caelestia*
entries.work: The Christian Theosophy of Böhme, Gichtel & Swedenborg
entries.creator: Emanuel Swedenborg
entries.summary:
The title page of *Arcana Caelestia* (Heavenly Secrets), Emanuel Swedenborg's vast Latin commentary on Genesis and Exodus, published anonymously in London from 1749.

## Iconography
A plain typographic title page in Latin, announcing the work that unfolds the 'heavenly secrets' contained in the internal sense of Scripture, together with wonders seen in the world of spirits and the heaven of angels.

## Significance
*Arcana Caelestia* is the foundation of Swedenborg's theology, expounding his doctrine of [[motif:correspondences of heaven and hell|correspondences]] verse by verse and reporting his experiences among angels and spirits. Issued without his name, it nonetheless became the seed of the New Church and a key text of modern esotericism. The sober title page embodies the work's character: not an illustrated revelation but a relentless, scholarly exegesis of the spiritual world.

## For artists & game designers
A lore-book prop: the unassuming title page of a multi-volume work that claims to decode the hidden meaning of every sacred text.
